coredata.dc:title: A new ratiometric fluorescent detection of Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase enzyme based on dually emitting carbon dots and silver nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Behzadifar, Shakila
Bagheri Pebdeni, Azam
Hosseini, Morteza
Mohammadnejad, Javad
coredata.dc:description: The defect of Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) is a considerable enzyme deficiency globally and causes many diseases. Here the ratiometric fluorescent method was designed for detecting G6PD based on silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and dually emitting carbon dots (DECDs) that exhibited dual emission fluorescence peaks in 470 and 523 (nm) under the single excitation wavelength at 330 nm. In the solution of mixed DECDs and AgNPs, the internal filter effect (IFE) causes a reduction of blue fluorescence of DECDs, because spectral overlapping happened between the emission of DECDs and the absorption of AgNPs (near 400 nm). In the absents of G6PD, the substrate of this enzyme (NADP+) was exited in the solution, causing the aggregation of AgNPs, and the absorption of aggregated AgNPs was shifted to about 520 nm. Therefore, recovering the blue fluorescence is happened. The ratiometric fluorometric assay is based on rationing the emissions (I470/I523). This method has a wide detection range (0.009–21 (U mL−1)) with a low detection limit of 0.006 (U mL−1). The assay was used to determine G6PD in spiked blood samples; the recovery values were obtained in the range of 96.19 % to 110 %. This cheap and fast method allows detecting G6PD on-site with visual detection by analyzing fluorescence color shades.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enzyme assay
Glucose −6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD)
Dual emissive carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescent
Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Process of detecting G6PD through ratiometric fluorescence
G6PD detection functions in blood samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to thank the Research Council of the University of Tehran for the financial support of this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image of DECDs, b) AgNPs, c) DECDs/AgNPs mixture (A magnified TEM image of DECDs/AgNPs mixture inset of Fig. 1.c), and d) DECDs/AgNPs mixture with the addition NADP+ in the absence of G6PD. e) FTIR spectrum of AgNPs, DECDs, and DECDs/AgNPs mixture.

a) EDX spectra of DECDs/AgNPs mixture, b) EDX elemental mapping of DECDs/AgNPs mixture and c) Average zeta potentials of AgNPs, DECDs, DECDs/AgNPs mixture in the presence of substances in the reaction with G6PD enzyme, and DECDs/AgNPs mixture in the substances in the reaction without the G6PD enzyme.

a) UV–vis absorption spectra of AgNPs in the presence of different concentrations of NADP+ (ranging from 0 to 10 mM). Inset demonstrates linearity over the range of 0 to 10 mM, each error bar shows the standard deviation of three replicate determinations, b) corresponding photographs under visible light. c) Fluorescent emission spectra of DECDs with the addition of AgNPs at different volume from 0 to 70 µL. (Inset a plotted linear relationship between the ratios (I470/I523) of fluorescent emission peak intensities versus different volume of AgNPs).

a) Fluorescent emission spectra of DECDs/AgNPs mixture in the presence of different concentrations (ranging from 0 to 21 U mL−1) of G6PD, 10.0 mM G6P,10.0 mM NADP+ were used. b) Demonstrated the linearity over the range of 0 to 21 U mL−1, each error bar shows the standard deviation of three replicate determinations. c) Visual fluorescent color under UV lamp (360 nm).

Consequences of the (I470/I523) of the DECDs/AgNPs mixture in the presence of several amino acids and different biomolecules with the concentration of NADP+ (10.0 mM) and all reaction materials compared with control (reaction material without disturbing agents).
Schematic description of the ratiometric fluorescent method for detecting Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase enzyme based on DECDs and AgNPs.
Recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) of G6PD in human RBC lysed samples.

head.ce:title: A new ratiometric fluorescent detection of Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase enzyme based on dually emitting carbon dots and silver nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanobiosensors lab, Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, University of Tehran, Tehran 1417614418, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanobiosensors lab, Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, University of Tehran, Tehran 1417614418, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medicinal Biomaterials Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medicinal Biomaterials Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, University of Tehran, Tehran 1417614418, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, University of Tehran, Tehran 1417614418, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Nanobiosensors lab, Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, University of Tehran, Tehran 1417614418, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The defect of Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) is a considerable enzyme deficiency globally and causes many diseases. Here the ratiometric fluorescent method was designed for detecting G6PD based on silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and dually emitting carbon dots (DECDs) that exhibited dual emission fluorescence peaks in 470 and 523 (nm) under the single excitation wavelength at 330 nm. In the solution of mixed DECDs and AgNPs, the internal filter effect (IFE) causes a reduction of blue fluorescence of DECDs, because spectral overlapping happened between the emission of DECDs and the absorption of AgNPs (near 400 nm). In the absents of G6PD, the substrate of this enzyme (NADP+) was exited in the solution, causing the aggregation of AgNPs, and the absorption of aggregated AgNPs was shifted to about 520 nm. Therefore, recovering the blue fluorescence is happened. The ratiometric fluorometric assay is based on rationing the emissions (I470/I523). This method has a wide detection range (0.009–21 (U mL−1)) with a low detection limit of 0.006 (U mL−1). The assay was used to determine G6PD in spiked blood samples; the recovery values were obtained in the range of 96.19 % to 110 %. This cheap and fast method allows detecting G6PD on-site with visual detection by analyzing fluorescence color shades.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase is a critical enzyme in red blood cells (RBCs) because it is the only producer of NADPH. This nucleotide, concurrently with glutathione reductase, maintains reduced glutathione (GSH); by this way, RBCs are protected from oxidative stress. G6PD deficiency is hereditable, with numerous prevailing polymorphisms and enzymopathy, influencing more than 400 million people worldwide [1,2]. Some people with this condition may evolve acute hemolysis initiated by taking fava beans, oxidant drugs, infections, and different unexplored exogenous agencies. In infants, lack of G6PD is associated with a grown risk of neonatal jaundice and bilirubin encephalopathy in some issues. The only way to prevent hemolysis is to make people aware of their defects and stay away from exogenous oxidative stress. [3,4]. The usual range of this enzyme varies. It is suggested that an individual laboratory launch its reference range to echo the population's age, sex, diet, and geographical location, depending on a person's hemoglobin. Hence, the enzyme activity is described in units per gram of hemoglobin (g Hb). The normal, intermediate, and deficient ranges of G6PD activity are reported as 4.0–13.0, 1.5–3.9, and 0.0 to 1.5 (U g Hb−1), respectively [5–7].

Various methods for measuring G6PD activity there generally based on detecting NADPH concentration indirectly [8]. Fluorescent and colorimetric techniques have acquired significant attention for detecting analytes due to their simplicity and lower price [9–12]. These methods are often used for detecting, which is not sensitive enough, and environmental factors affect the result. Today, nanoparticles play an essential role in designing diagnostic devices [13–18]. Nano-based sensors for detecting this enzyme are limited [19]. Colorimetric methods have been designed to measure this enzyme using metal nanoparticles such as silver and gold [20,21].

Nowadays, fluorescent sensors are used efficiently; numerous probes based on fluorescence are established on turning on or off principles, which are influenced by exterior characteristics and cause false-positive results. Fluorescence sensing-based single intensity has some disadvantages, including local concentration divergence of the probe, instrumental elements such as excitation origin fluctuation, light scattering by the sampling matrix, and a specific microenvironment close to the probe [22–25]. Compared to this technique, fluorescence probes based on ratiometric could eliminate mistakes driven by surroundings or apparatuses, making the outcomes more precise [26,27]. Alternatively, fluorescent chemo/bio-fluorescent probes acquire attention to meaningful sensor values, such as lower expense, operating plainness, real-time analyzing, and improving the sensitivity and selectivity of detection [28].

Due to the importance of enzyme detection, cheap, accurate, and fast methods are required. Fluorescent chemo/bio probes with single fluorescent output cannot accurately detect analytes in biological samples due to signal fluctuations and intrinsic background fluorescence [29–32]. In the ratiometric fluorescence methods, using the ratio of two separate intensity emission wavelengths rather than measuring the intensity of one wavelength to analyze the sensing process makes it more accurate and precise [33]. The dual ratiometric system applies dual signals, supporting self-calibration for intensities of signals to evade the impact of surroundings signals and target-independent elements. Using ratiometric dual-signal outcome in a chemo/bio fluorescent probe approach improves detection sensitivity and precision [34–36]. Up to now, no information has been reported about chemo/bio fluorescence probes for ratiometric detection of G6PD. Carbon dots (CDs) have remarkable characteristics, including lower toxicity, biocompatibility, low cost, chemical inactivity, uncomplicated preparation, benign chemical composition, tunable fluorescence emission, facile functionalization, perfect physicochemical and photochemical stability, attracting scientists ares have a widely used in profitable utilization such as chem/bio fluorescent sensing, biological imaging, photodynamic treatment, etc [37–41].

In this work, for the first time, the G6PD enzyme has been detected using CDs and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs); CDs have been used, which, unlike most CDs, have a simple synthesis, and materials such as biotiols and heavy metals have not been used in this method. Yunxia Ma and colleagues first used this carbon dot to measure chromium (VI) [42]. The carbon dot showed dual emission fluorescence peaks (470 nm and 523 nm) under the single excitation wavelength of 330 nm and had acceptable water solubility and remarkable fluorescence. The strategy is to have a clear overlap between the blue diffusion spectrum of dually emitting carbon dots (DECDs) and the absorption spectrum of AgNPs dispersed in water. The DECDs emitted blue and green fluorescence peaked at 435 nm and 520 nm. By adding AgNPs into DECDs' solution, a significant overlap was observed between the blue-emission spectra of DECDs and the absorption spectra of AgNPs. Blue fluorescence reduction occurred due to the internal filter effect (IFE). NADP+ is a substrate of G6PD; in the absence of this enzyme, NADP+ is present because of electrostatic attraction between the BH4− group around AgNPs and the positive charge of NADP+, AgNPs were aggregated. Thus, blue fluorescence is recovered in the absence of the G6PD enzyme by adding DECDs/AgNPs. These particular facets promote investigation as a new dual-ratiometry system for precise detection of G6PD (Scheme 1 ).

All chemicals acquired have the analytical grade without extra modification and purification. The water utilized in experimentations is ultrapure. Oxalic acid dihydrate, m–aminophenol (98 %), NaBH4, and AgNO3 were received from Merck Millipore (Burlington, MA, USA). G6PD enzymes from S. cerevisiae, NADP+, and D-G6P disodium salt hydrate were bought from Sigma-Aldrich.

Perkin-Elmer LS50 luminescence Spectrometer with a xenon light source was used for all fluorescence measures; for excitation and emission, the slit width was arranged at 5 nm. All absorption analysis was taken on A PerkinElmer (Waltham, MA, USA) Lambda 2 spectrometer. TEM images were taken by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Philips EM208S 100KV). A dynamic light scattering spectrophotometer (Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern devices, Malvern, UK) was utilized for examined the size potential. IS50 FTIR (Nicolet) apparatus was operated for measuring Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The Philips XL30 scanning electron microscope (SEM) analyzed the samples' energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra. A dark box equipped with a 360 nm UV lamp for excitation was utilized for visualizing detect fluorescent colors.

DECDs were synthesized via m-aminophenol and oxalic acid (54.5 and 32.0 (mg), respectively). Firstly, 5 mL of ultrapure water was used for solving the materials. Then, a heated bath was used to obtain a uniform solution under vigorous stirring. After that mixture was repositioned into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave for heating for 12 h at 180° C in an oven. Finally, the solution was cooled, and a solution with yellow color was acquired. Afterward, the 0.22 μm membrane filter vacated the large particles from the solution [43]. For synthesized AgNPs, silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), and distilled water were utilized. For stabilizing AgNPs and reducing the ionic silver, NaBH4 was required. First, the 30.0 mL of 2.0 mM NaBH4 was prepared and stirred on a magnetic stirrer plate with the ice bath; second, 10.0 mL of 1.0 mM AgNO3 was added in drops to the NaBH4 solution. Eventually, The solution shifted to light yellow after adding 2.0 mL of AgNO3 [44,45].

First, NADP+ with different concentrations from 0 to 10 mM was added to vials, then silver nanoparticles with a volume of 100 µL were added to each vial. The solution was put at room temperature for 5 min; then, to analyze the absorbance spectra of the solutions, a UV–VIS spectrophotometer was utilized at 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Process of detecting G6PD through ratiometric fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: AgNPs were added from 0 to 70 µL to the DECDs solution under magnetic stirring to obtain a uniform mixture; after that, the solution was incubated in the dark for 10 min. By increasing AgNPs concentration, the fluorescent emission spectra of solutions were studied under excitation at 330 nm. Fluorescent intensity peaked at 470, blue-emitting was reduced. A solution containing 70 µL AgNPs was selected for detecting G6PD. Then, the reaction in the assay, including two substrates of the enzyme (10.0 mM NADP+ and 10.0 mM G6P), 10.0 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.5) for adjustment of pH, 10.0 mM MgCl2 as a cofactor for enzyme activity, and G6PD enzyme concentration range from 0 to 21 (U mL-1) was prepared. The mixture was allowed to proceed for 15 min at ambient temperature; then, a 100 µL mix of AgNPs and DECDs (AgNPs/DECDs) was added to the mixtures. Fluorescent intensities peaked at 470, and 523 nm were converted into a ratiometry of fluorescent (I470/I523) by increasing the enzyme concentration. A good relationship between I470/I523 and G6PD was observed, creating a ratiometric fluorescent method for detecting G6PD.

To select the optimal circumstances for the required factors, different material concentrations were evaluated to determine the optimal concentration. First, the concentration of glucose-6-phosphate from 4 − 19 mM was analyzed; after that, optimization of NADP+ was studied by estimating the impacts of different concentrations (range of 4–16 mM). The incubation time between the enzyme mixture and AgNPs/DECD was picked by examining the time between 0 and 20 min. The effect of the buffer pH was discussed (range of 6.5 – 8.5), and finally, the acceptable volume of AgNPs/DECD for adding was examined (range of 20 – 120 µL).

The morphology and size of the DECDs and AgNPs were studied by TEM. As represented in Fig. 1 . TEM images of DECDs (Fig. 1a) indicate monodisperse, uniform, and rounded particles, which have an average diameter of about 4.5 nm. Fig. 1b illustrated AgNPs with an average size between 10 and 20 nm; The TEM image was shown that particles are spherical and dispersed well in the solution. After adding AgNPs to DECD, the aggregation of a number of small particles of AgNPs was detectable (Fig. 1c); this aggregation happened due to electrostatic interactions between BH4 − around AgNPs and groups around DECDs with a negative charge such as –OH, –NH2, or –COOH. In the mixture of DECDs/AgNPs, adding NADP+ caused an electrostatic attraction between BH4− around AgNPs and NADP+, so some AgNPs' aggregation occurred. (Fig. 1d). The FTIR results affirmed the chemical bonds and group's function that attended in the samples. Fig. 1e illustrates that the absorption peaks at 3490, 3921, and 1597 cm−1 come from the stretching vibrations of –OH, –NH2, and CO bonds, respectively. The absorption peak at 1498 and 1155 cm−1 is allocated to the CC bond stretching vibration and bending vibration of the C–NH bond, respectively. CO bond vibration was attributed to the absorption peak at 964 cm−1. Alternatively, the presence of two peaks at 1498 and 1072 (cm−1) defined the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration (asymmetric and symmetric, respectively) of CO, respectively [42]. In the FTIR spectrum of AgNPs, a peak at 3720 cm−1 shows stretching of the alcohol group with a strong peak. 1095 cm−1 corresponds to the NH bond. The presence of –OH groups was confirmed by A distinct broad peak at 698 cm−1 [45,46]. Alternatively, in Fig. 1e, FTIR of the mixture of AgNPs/DECDs was displayed, indicating that the two materials have interacted with each other. A new probe containing this de-material has been developed. The sum of the AgNPs and DECDs peaks can be seen. There is also a slight change in the sharp peaks at 1087 and 671 due to the interaction between AgNPs and DECDs; a small number of silver nanoparticles have aggregated in this interaction.

SEM-based Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDX) elemental mapping was utilized to examine the elemental composition of AgNPs/DECDs. It verified the presence of B, C, N, O, and Ag with an abundance of 4.2, 55.05, 20.66, 18.56,1.53, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The elemental mapping exhibits a uniform distribution of B, C, N, O, and Ag in the AgNPs/DECDs authorizes the existence of these elements, the outcomes were demonstrated in Fig. 2b. Zeta potentials of AgNPs, DECDs, AgNPs/DECDs in the presence and absence of G6PD were studied, as illustrated in Fig. 2c. Their average zeta potentials were estimated to be −28.6, −0.97, −9.46, and 6.23, respectively. DECDs have a weak, negatively charged characteristic related to m–aminophenol containing –OH and –NH2 and oxalic acid containing –COOH groups. Weak negatively charged of DECDs mostly was related to –COO–. The negative charge of AgNPs was due to BH4 − in the presence of G6PD, AgNPs remain stable, and the negative surface charge of AgNPs/DECDs is stable. However, in the absence of G6PD, NADP+ has existed in the solution, and the average zeta potential of mixture AgNPs/DECDs is positive due to the aggregation of AgNPs.

The optical properties of AgNPs and DECDs were evaluated under various conditions. AgNPs was displayed UV–vis absorption peaks near 400 nm (Fig.S1.a). The UV–vis absorption of DECDs showed a peak at 280 nm, related to the π-π* transition of the CC group. The two absorption bands of DECDs, around 345 and 390 (nm), retaining electron transitions and the corresponding functional groups were shown in Fig. S1.b. The fluorescence emission spectra of the DECDs with various excitation wavelengths were studied (from 310 to 370 nm), and the outcomes are shown in Fig.S1.c. DECDs were shown maximum emission at 470 and 523 (nm), with a good intensity near 800 under 330 nm excitation (Fig.S1.d).

AgNPs have high reactivity due to the ratio of surface area to volume. Electrostatic repulsion and steric repulsion both of them are the mechanisms that limit adherence to colloidal particles: in the synthesizing of AgNPs, NaBH4 caused stability of nanoparticles by is used by creating a negative charge around them [44]; The enzyme produces NADPH from NADP+. When the enzyme is not present in the environment, the substrate's concentration is high, affecting the AgNPs and their aggregated [21]. Initially, its effect on AgNPs was investigated separately. Redshifted appeared due to a decline in the intensity of the plasmon absorbance, and a new peak emerged around 520 nm. It is evaluated from 0 to 10 NADP+ in 100 mL of AgNPs. The results are visible in Fig. 3 a. the standard curve was gained (from 0 to 10 mM) using the linear regression equation Y = -0.057 X + 0.8441 (R2 = 0.9705) by raising the NADP+ from 0 to 10 (mM), the soluble coloring of the solution alters from light yellow grayish to pale pink and then grayish (Fig. 3b). By adding AgNPs into DECD, the mixture solution was prepared. Increasing AgNPs cause changes in the fluorescent spectral, demonstrating dramatically reduced fluorescent peaks at 470 and 523 nm. This phenomenon has happened due to the absorption of AgNPs and emission spectra of DECDs. Blue fluorescent reduction of DECDs appeared because of the IFE phenomenon; there is considerable overlapping between the blue-emission spectra of DECDs (470 nm) and the absorption spectra of AgNPs (around 400 nm). The process of forming the DECDs/AgNPs caused the slight aggregation of AgNPs, so red-shifted absorption of AgNPs has happened. When redshifted, the absorption spectra of aggregated AgNPs had considerable overlap with the green-emission spectra of DECDs (around 523 nm). IFE caused the green fluorescent slip of DECDs to occur. There was an excellent linear association between (I470/I523) and the increased volume of AgNPs. The linear regression equation is Y = -0.0123X + 1.2834, in which Y is (I470/I523), X is the volume of AgNPs in µL; also, R2 = 0.9874 is adequate; the outcomes are shown in Fig. 3c. With the increase of AgNPs from 0 to 120 µL, fluorescent colors alter from deep blue to deep green. In the solution of DECDs/AgNPs, the fluorescent intensity was changed by adding a mixture of reactions, including G6P, NADP+, Tris, MgCl2, and G6PD. The presence or absence of G6PD enzymes in the environment causes a change in the substrate of this enzyme (NADP+), which affects the AgNPs, and, ultimately, reduces fluorescent peaks at 523 nm and increased fluorescent peaks at 470 nm have happened.

The influences of different parameters, such as the concentration of two substrates (G6P and NADP+), pH, various incubation times, and volume of DECDs/AgNPs added to the reaction solution, were studied and optimized. First, the concentrations of G6P (4,7,10,13,16 and 19 mM) were analyzed (Fig.S2.a); no significant differences were observed in (I470/I523); however, at concentration 10 mM, suitable intensities were shown. Then the concentration of NADP+ (4,7,10,13 and 16 mM) was studied the highest ratio intensities (I470/I523) were achieved at a concentration of 10.0 mM (Fig.S2.b). The affection of pH in the range of 6.5–8.5 was examined (Fig.S2.c). Nanoparticles were stable, and the G6PD enzyme indicates excellent activity at a pH of 7.5. G6PD had less activity at the lower pH, and the nanoparticles no longer precipitated at a pH above 7.5. To evaluate the florescent stability of DECDs/AgNPs with the addition of G6PD were estimated at various incubation times from 0 to 20 min. Lengthening time to 20 min shows a subtle change in ratio intensities (I470/I523), which the better intensities had in 5 min incubation (Fig.S2.d). The volume of DECDs/AgNPs added to the reaction solution was analyzed as a final parameter. From a concentration range of 20 –120 µL, sound ratio intensities (I470/I523) were observed in the volume of 100 µL. At volumes above 100, the intensity of the fluorescent spectrum increases and passes through 1000, and at low volume, the fluorescent ratio intensities (I470/I523) were not appropriate (Fig.S2.e).

Subsequently, the efficiency of the AgNPs and DECDs for detecting G6PD was investigated in ideal circumstances. Different concentrations of the G6PD enzyme were studied to evaluate the assay's sensitivity. Results demonstrated in Fig. 4 a, by reducing the G6PD, NADP+ as enzymes' substrate has existed in the medium; due to the positive charge of the substrate and the negative charge around the AgNPs, the aggregation of AgNPs has happened in the absence of G6PD. In other words, absorption–emission spectral overlapping features between DECDs and AgNPs caused this mechanism, and by aggregated the AgNPs, the blue fluorescent is recovered. There is an excellent linear association between (I470/I523) and G6PD in the various concentrations from 0 to 21 (U mL−1); the calibration curve also was achieved in that concentrations utilizing the linear regression formula Y = 0.0048X + 0.9112 (R2 = 0.9895), which Y is (I470/I523), and X is equal to the concentration of G6PD in (U mL−1), the outcomes can be seen in Fig. 4b. The limit of detection (LOD) of the suggested technique for G6PD was calculated to be 0.006 (U mL−1), which showed this method has adequate sensitivity (S/N = 3). Visual fluorescent color changing can be observed under a UV lamp (360 nm); with the decreasing of G6PD from 0 to 21 (U mL−1), there are shining, normal, and dissimilar fluorescent colors altering from deep green to colorless (Fig. 4c).

The essential factor is a diagnosis device's proficiency in detecting analytes from other materials in the solution. An efficient device in interfering factors could also show acceptable performance. Since the blood sample has many complexities, several materials, including some amino acids (Cys, Tyr, Trp, and Met) and ascorbic acid, urea, Na+, and K + in existence of all reaction materials were analyzed under the same circumstances. The Disturbing materials concentration was similar to the substrate of the enzyme (NADP+ with the concentration of 10 mM) that was added to the reaction mixture. The graph in Fig. 5 did not exhibit much difference in intensity (I470/I523); These results also confirmed this assay's high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: G6PD detection functions in blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: RBCs were used to evaluate the functions of this work in the real sample because the G6PD enzyme is present in these cells, so this aim, patients' blood with deficiency of G6PDwas received from the Massoud Clinical Diagnosis Laboratory. Foremost for preparing the sample based on commercial kits' protocol, RBCs were diluted (25 times) with lysate solution and allowed 10 min at room temperature for hemolyzing RBCs. After that, the sample was diluted (30 times) with water and, for 10 min, was centrifuged at 6000 rpm. Eventually, diverse concentrations of G6PD were spiked into diluted blood. The outcomes of the study are shown in Table 1 . Recovery values indicated promising results in the range of 96.19 % to 110 %, with sufficient RSD values (1.80–3.20 %), which signifies that the suggested work possible has a good function for G6PD detection in real biological samples.

To sum it up, in this work, for the first time, a novel fluorescence probe based on ratiometric established on DECDs and AgNPs was created to sensitively detection of the G6PD enzyme based on Unite/mL. Considering the importance of detecting homozygous or heterozygous for this enzyme in the clinical laboratory, this method can quickly identify a suitable range. DECDs emitted blue and green fluorescence by adding AgNPs to DECDs solution; IFE has happened cause the blue fluorescence reduction of DECDs. In the absence of G6PD, its substrate NADP+ was exited in the environment, causing silver nanoparticle aggregation and red-shifted absorption. This phenomenon caused the blue-emission fluorescence recovery of DECDs. In blood samples, DECDs/AgNPs solution detected G6PD with heightened recoveries and lower RSD. This simplistic and straightforward strategy of fluorescence probes can allow further evolution of optical detection of analytes, which are essential for easy, quick, and efficient detection and suitable candidates for clinical diagnostics.

Shakila Behzadifar designed and performed the experiments and also wrote the manuscript. Azam Bagheri Pebdeni reviewed the manuscript. Morteza Hosseini, as the corresponding author, designed experiments, sponsored the study and made all the arrangements. Javad Mohammadnejad sponsored the study. All the authors reviewed the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Research Council of the University of Tehran for the financial support of this study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107947.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

Medicine & Health
